title: Small Old English Style Farmhouse Dining Table
summary: A compact farmhouse table on turned 3x3 legs, built with a pocket-hole apron and a planked 1x6 top. Seats four to six.
description:
This is the farmhouse table stripped to its essentials: four store-bought turned legs, a simple 1x4/1x2 apron, and a top glued and screwed up from six 1x6 boards. Buying the turned legs is what makes it look 'old English' without a lathe — Osborne and similar suppliers sell them in a range of profiles, and the whole character of the table rides on which one you pick.

The apron is joined to the legs with pocket screws set 1/4" in from the outside face, so the leg reveals a clean shoulder rather than sitting flush. Cross-bracing cut to fit in the corners stiffens the base and gives the tabletop screws something to bite into. The 1x2 lower trim wraps the apron and reads as a second, thinner rail — it's cosmetic, but it's what separates this from a plain four-legs-and-a-box table.

At 55" the top is on the small side, which is the point: it fits a breakfast nook or a small dining room and can be cut in half across the boards for transport if you build it somewhere other than where it lives. Sand the top thoroughly and finish for the wear a dining surface takes.

tools.note: The whole base and top go together with pocket screws.
Handy for breaking down the 10-ft top boards.
A dining top needs to be sanded smooth — budget real time here.

materials.name: Turned legs, 3 in x 3 in
materials.note: Store-bought (e.g. Osborne Wood). The leg profile sets the whole look.

materials.name: 1x6, 10 ft
materials.note: Tabletop boards — can be cut in half for transport.

materials.name: Finish (stain and topcoat)
materials.note: A durable topcoat — this is an eating surface.

cutList.note: Cut to fit the corners — 33" is approximate.

steps.title: Pocket-hole and attach the end aprons
steps.body:
Drill 3/4" pocket holes along all four edges of each end apron. The top and bottom edges are for attaching the tabletop and the lower trim; the ends are for joining to the legs.

Attach the end aprons to the legs with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue, inset 1/4" from the outside face of the leg. That 1/4" reveal is deliberate — it gives a clean shoulder instead of a flush, furniture-of-mystery joint.

steps.title: Attach the side aprons and corner braces
steps.body:
Repeat the pocket-hole layout on the side aprons and join them to the leg assemblies, again inset 1/4" and with glue.

Cut cross braces from 1x4 material to fit diagonally across each corner and screw them in. They stiffen the base against racking and give you solid wood to drive the tabletop screws into later.

steps.title: Add the lower trim
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes in the ends of the 1x2 lower trim pieces. Attach them to the legs with 1-1/4" pocket screws, then fasten them up into the bottom edge of the aprons through the pocket holes you drilled earlier. This gives the base its two-rail farmhouse look and hides the apron's lower pocket holes.

steps.title: Build the top and fasten it to the base
steps.body:
Glue and clamp up the six 1x6 boards into a top, keeping the joints tight and the surface flat. If you're joining with pocket screws, drive them from underneath so nothing shows on top.

Once the top is dry and sanded, center it on the base and screw up through the apron pocket holes into the underside with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue. Sand through the grits and finish with a durable topcoat.

images.alt: Small farmhouse dining table with turned legs and a planked pine top
